mission.title: Install and Configure Prometheus Operator / kube-prometheus-stack
mission.description: The Prometheus Operator simplifies the deployment and management of Prometheus monitoring stacks on Kubernetes using custom resources. It is ideal for users looking to automate monitoring configurations and manage Prometheus instances effectively. Key trade-offs include the need for familiarity with Kubernetes custom resources and potential complexity in advanced configurations.

steps.title: Add the Prometheus Operator Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Prometheus Operator Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts

helm repo update

```

> This is the official repository for Prometheus Operator Helm charts.
> Updates your local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the Prometheus Operator
steps.description:
This command installs the Prometheus Operator using Helm, specifying the namespace and version.

```bash

kubectl create namespace monitoring

helm install prometheus-operator prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack --namespace monitoring --version 0.75.0

```

> Creates a namespace for the monitoring stack.
> Installs the kube-prometheus-stack with a specific version.

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Prometheus Operator and its components are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n monitoring

kubectl get svc -n monitoring

```

> Lists all pods in the monitoring namespace to verify their status.
> Lists all services in the monitoring namespace to ensure they are created.

steps.title: Create a Prometheus custom resource
steps.description:
This step creates a sample Prometheus custom resource to start monitoring your applications.

```bash

cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: Prometheus
metadata:
  name: example-prometheus
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  replicas: 1
  serviceAccountName: prometheus-operator
  podMonitorSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app
EOF

```

> Creates a basic Prometheus instance.
commands:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: Prometheus
metadata:
  name: example-prometheus
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  replicas: 1
  serviceAccountName: prometheus-operator
  podMonitorSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app
EOF

uninstall.title: Uninstall the Prometheus Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Prometheus Operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all monitoring data and configurations.

```bash

helm uninstall prometheus-operator --namespace monitoring

kubectl delete namespace monitoring

```

> Uninstalls the Prometheus Operator.
> Deletes the monitoring namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Prometheus Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your Prometheus data and configurations. This step will upgrade the Prometheus Operator to the specified version.

```bash

kubectl get prometheus -n monitoring -o yaml > prometheus-backup.yaml

helm upgrade prometheus-operator prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack --namespace monitoring --version 0.76.0

kubectl get pods -n monitoring

```

> Backs up the current Prometheus custom resource configuration.
> Upgrades the Prometheus Operator to the new version.
> Verifies that all pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get prometheus -n monitoring -o yaml > prometheus-backup.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Error creating service: services "prometheus-operated" already exists'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This error occurs when a service with the same name already exists in the namespace.

**Fix:**
Delete the existing service using `kubectl delete svc prometheus-operated -n monitoring` and retry the installation.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to pull image ...'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the specified container image cannot be found or accessed.

**Fix:**
Check the image name in your configuration and ensure it is available in the specified container registry.

troubleshooting.title: Pod is in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually indicates that the application inside the pod is crashing due to misconfiguration or resource limits.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs using `kubectl logs <pod-name> -n monitoring` to identify the issue.

troubleshooting.title: Prometheus is not scraping targets
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the service discovery configuration is incorrect or the target pods are not labeled properly.

**Fix:**
Verify the labels on your target pods and ensure they match the `podMonitorSelector` in your Prometheus custom resource.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for additional metrics exporters and improved Grafana dashboards.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the use of legacy API versions for custom resources.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v0.76, ensure to update your custom resources to use the new API version.

resolution.summary: You should see the Prometheus UI accessible via the service created in the monitoring namespace, and the output of `kubectl get prometheus -n monitoring` should show your Prometheus instance with a status of 'Running'.
codeSnippets:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: Prometheus
metadata:
  name: example-prometheus
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  replicas: 1
  serviceAccountName: prometheus-operator
  podMonitorSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary tools installed.
